summary:
Late postoperative endocarditis typically results from Viridans group streptococci; while staphylococcal infections are possible earlier after surgery, they become less common as time progresses past one month. Thus, \(\boxed{\text{viridans-streptococci}}\) represents the primary cause according to current medical understanding based on multiple studies reviewing this condition over various durations post-surgery.
 The summary captures both critical aspects—time frame relevance and specific bacterial causes—and directly addresses why Viridans group strep stands out as significant compared to other potential pathogens mentioned initially but deemed secondary given their predominance at longer intervals relative to surgical procedures. Agent
 To summarize effectively within two concise statements focused solely on clinically pertinent details related to identifying the bacterium causing post-operative endocarditis:

Post-operative endocarditis usually occurs more than six weeks after surgery and predominantly involves *Viridans-group Streptococci*. This contrasts sharply with initial infection periods where *Staphylococcus aureus* tends to predominate instead. Hence, recognizing timing helps pinpoint appropriate etiological factors accurately. 

This version maintains clarity around temporal distinctions impacting pathogen selection without extraneous detail, ensuring alignment strictly with core diagnostic considerations highlighted originally yet condensed into precisely required brevity.
